Tafseer:
1. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(By the star when it descends) (1) - The meaning of 'Al-Najm': Commentators have generally taken 'Al-Najm' (the star) to refer to the Pleiades, but there is no explicit context for this. A clearer context could have been Sirius, which is mentioned later in this Surah, but there is no place for that meaning here either. In our view, it is used as a generic noun, just as in: (And by the stars they guide themselves) (An-Nahl, 16:16) or: (And the stars and trees prostrate) (Ar-Rahman, 55:6), and other similar verses in the Qur'an.
The meaning of 'hawa yahwee': The root meaning of 'hawa' is to fall from above. This word is appropriate both for describing the setting and disappearing of stars from the horizon, and for the pelting of shooting stars at the devils who try to overhear the secrets of the unseen, as mentioned in various places of the Qur'an. In 'Wa-n-najmi', the 'wa' is for an oath, and we repeatedly explain in this book that oaths in the Qur'an serve as testimonies to a claim.

(Your companion has not strayed, nor has he erred, nor does he speak from [his own] desire. It is not but a revelation revealed) (2-4).
Refuting the accusation of fabrication against the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): This serves as the subject of the oath (muqsam 'alayh). Swearing by the setting or falling of the stars, He addressed the Quraysh: your companion (the Prophet, peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) has neither strayed nor erred. The klam (speech) he recites to you is not fabricated of his own accord, but is revealed to him by Allah so that he recites it to you to guide you.
The context indicates that this refutes the accusation of soothsaying the Quraysh leveled against the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). When he recited the Qur'an and shared his experiences of the revelation and the angel, the leaders of the Quraysh tried to convince their people that he was like their soothsayers and astrologers, who presented rhymed and rhythmic speech based on their observations of the stars and the casting of the jinn. They claimed his assertion that an angel brought him revelation was a mere threat, asserting that just as the jinn cast words upon soothsayers, a jinni cast words upon him, whom he mistook for an angel.
This accusation is refuted in various places in the Qur'an, particularly at the end of Surah Ash-Shu'ara. Here, it is refuted through a different approach, starting with the oath by the setting and falling of the stars.
The testimony of the setting and falling of the stars: Through this, the Qur'an dealt a blow to two aspects of the pre-Islamic beliefs regarding astrologers and soothsayers. First, the sun, moon, stars, and planets do not possess any independent authority or power to benefit or harm; rather, they are subservient to the Creator of the universe. Their rising and setting in a highly disciplined system bear witness that they possess no independent authority; therefore, they are neither worthy of worship nor of being regarded as the source of revelation and prophecy, nor of being called upon in times of calamity, nor of being sought for blessings. Rather, they prostrate to Allah and invite His servants to do the same. This is expressed in various ways in the Qur'an, and Abraham's argument against his people is the definitive proof in this regard.
Note that soothsaying was closely linked both to the jinn and to the movements of the stars. The Qur'an dealt a blow to this by: 'By the star when it descends'—showing that the stars themselves bear witness that they are subject to the Creator, rising and setting by His command; so foolish are those who try to seek guidance or determine destinies from them, and even more foolish are those who call the Prophet an astrologer or soothsayer, when his entire teaching deals a blow to these myths. Second, the soothsayers' claim that they receive news of the unseen from the jinn is false. No one has access to the unseen; if any devil tries to overhear, His power has arranged that shooting stars are pelted at them. In Surah As-Saffat, He states:
(Except him who snatches a hearing, then there pursues him a burning flame) (As-Saffat, 37:10). The jinn themselves confessed this:
(And we used to sit therein in positions for hearing, but whoever listens now will find a burning flame lying in wait for him) (Al-Jinn, 72:9). Referencing these falling stars, Allah Almighty declared the Qur'an completely pure from any satanic influence in Surah Al-Waqi'ah (56:75-78), Surah Al-Haqqah (69:38-43), and Surah At-Takwir (81:15-25)—showing that no devil can reach the Preserved Tablet, nor influence the noble angel who brings the revelation, nor mislead the Prophet. Devils descend only on those soothsayers who are liars and perform tricks to deceive people.
Explanation of some words: Having explained the relationship of the oath, let us understand the words. 'Dall' (strayed) is used for the error of thought or oversight, and 'ghawa' (erred) is used for the error of intention and desires. 'Sahib' (companion) refers to the Prophet, and the addressees are the Quraysh: 'Your companion is not a stranger to you. He was born and raised among you, and you have been witnesses to his nobility, truthfulness, and trustworthiness. You never witnessed any inclination to soothsaying in him, which could not have remained hidden; so why do you call him a soothsayer now when he recites the Qur'an, when his life and speech are free from any error.' 'Nor does he speak from [his own] desire' (wa ma yantiqu 'ani-l-hawa) - 'an' points to the source—meaning: this speech has no connection with his desires, but is revealed by Allah for your guidance. This is a rebuke to soothsayers, whose speech is produced by their own desires.
'The wine of this cup is superior to all.'
While this refers primarily to the Qur'an, as clarified by: 'It is not but a revelation revealed' (in huwa illa wahyuy-yuha), since the Prophet is protected from error and his conduct is a model, his general life is also pure, and if any minor lapse occurred, Allah Almighty corrected it immediately.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 2, 3, and 4 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 2, 3, and 4 was combined in the original text.]

(Taught him by one mighty in power) (5)
The attributes of Gabriel: Having described the speech and its presenter, this describes the attributes of the angel (Gabriel) who taught him. It is stated: he is 'mighty in power' (shadeed-ul-quwa)—meaning he is equipped with the highest physical and spiritual capabilities in their perfect form. There is no possibility of any other spirit overcoming or influencing him, or committing any betrayal in the revelation, or causing any error or whisper. Allah has protected him from all weaknesses so that he executes his assigned duty with complete honesty and trustworthiness, as described in Surah At-Takwir: (Indeed, it is the word of a noble messenger, possessed of power, with the Owner of the Throne, secure [in position], obeyed there [in the heavens] and trustworthy) (81:19-21). 'Dhu mirrah' (endued with wisdom) (verse 6) means he is highly mature in his intellect and character, and cannot be deceived. This is an expression of his moral and intellectual superiority. Note that these attributes are mentioned both to devalue the soothsayers' source of inspiration and to refute the Jews and their followers who accused the angel of betrayal and bias—the animosity toward Gabriel is referenced in the Qur'an.

(Endued with wisdom. So he stood poised, while he was on the high horizon. Then he approached and let himself down, until he was two-bows' length off or even closer. And He revealed to His servant what He revealed) (6-10)
The manner of Gabriel's teaching: 'Dhu mirrah' relates to 'shadeed-ul-quwa', which was explained. Now, 'fa-stawa' (so he stood poised) explains the manner of teaching referred to in 'taught him'. It is stated: this مقرب (near) angel taught the Prophet with great care, attention, and compassion. 'Fa' is for explanation—meaning: first, he appeared in his actual, upright form on the high horizon. 'The high horizon' (al-ufuq-al-a'la) refers to the horizon directly overhead. Anything appearing overhead is visible clearly and without any confusion, unlike something appearing on the eastern or western horizon. This points to the reality that Moses saw Gabriel on the horizon clearly, without any confusion.
'Then he approached and let himself down' (thumma dana fa-tadalla) - 'tadalla' means to bend or incline toward someone. This describes how Gabriel approached the Prophet to teach him, leaning toward him with the compassion and affection a noble teacher shows to his beloved student. That is: he did not merely speak from a distance, without caring whether the Prophet heard or understood, but spoke with such care and closeness that he could hear and understand. Note that the devils' teaching is described as 'snatching' (khatafah), and they teach their soothsayers in like manner; hence, the Qur'an highlighted Gabriel's manner of teaching to show the difference. 'until he was two-bows' length off or even closer' (fa-kana qaba qawsayni aw adna) - 'qab' means measure, and this is a metaphor for extreme closeness and proximity. It is stated: he became so close to the Prophet that only a measure of two bows or even less remained between them. This metaphor is suited to the taste of the Arabs, who used bows and arrows and expressed closeness through the measure of bows. 'Aw' (or) indicates that the distance can be even less. 'And He revealed to His servant what He revealed' (fa-awha ila 'abdihi ma awha) - the subject of 'awha' (revealed) can be Allah or Gabriel. In the first case, Allah Almighty revealed to His servant—the Messenger—what He willed. In the second case, the pronoun refers to Allah, meaning: Gabriel revealed to Allah's servant what He directed him to reveal. I prefer the first interpretation, though the second is also correct. Some have derived the incorrect conclusion that Allah named the Prophet the servant of Gabriel. We repeatedly explain that the reference of pronouns is determined by context, and the change of reference is a part of Arabic eloquence.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 was combined in the original text.]

(The heart lied not in what he saw) (11)
Confirming the Prophet's vision: This is the confirmation of the Prophet's vision from Allah—showing that no one should regard this vision as a delusion of his mind or a trick; rather, he indeed had this vision.
We noted that when the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) mentioned his visions, the opponents mocked him, claiming his desires made him see these things as dreams, which he presented to people as reality, whereas it was a delusion. The Qur'an refuted this in various places, and it will be discussed under Surah At-Takwir.

(Will you then dispute with him as to what he saw?) (12) - This rebukes the opponents: do you dispute with the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) regarding what he saw? He is informing you of what he saw with his eyes and heard with his ears; if it is not visible to you, it does not negate the reality. Note that these opponents believed the nonsense of their soothsayers because it suited their desires, but opposed the Prophet's call because it was contrary to their desires.

(And he certainly saw him in another descent, at the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary, near it is the Garden of Refuge) (13-15)
(The second vision): That is: he did not have this vision only once, such that it could be regarded as a delusion; rather, he saw Gabriel in his actual form a second time at the Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary (Sidrat-ul-Muntaha), near which is the Garden of Refuge. The meaning is: what he is describing to you is a solid reality to be considered seriously, not a joke. Note that referencing only two visions was to answer those who mocked initially, and does not mean he saw Gabriel only twice. After these two initial visions, Gabriel's appearance occurred repeatedly in different forms and at different times, until his arrival was well known and beloved to the Prophet.
(Sidrat-ul-Muntaha): This is the boundary where the physical world ends. 'Sidrah' means a lote tree, and this tree represents the boundary between the physical and spiritual worlds. This belongs to the unseen world, and we cannot comprehend its actual reality. It is from the allegorical verses; therefore, we must maintain general belief in it without prying into its actual reality, which is known only to Allah. Those firm in knowledge increase in their knowledge through these, while those who try to pry into their reality stumble.
(near it is the Garden of Refuge): This defines the location of the Lote Tree, showing that near it is the Garden of Refuge (Jannat-ul-Ma'wa), which was discussed under Surah As-Sajdah, verse 19. This shows that the second vision occurred at the connection between the physical and spiritual worlds.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 13, 14, and 15 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 13, 14, and 15 was combined in the original text.]

(When there covered the Lote Tree that which covered [it]) (16)
(The quality of the vision is beyond description): This describes the quality of the vision—showing that the Lote Tree was covered in an abundance of divine lights and manifestations that cannot be described in words.

(The sight did not swerve, nor did it transgress) (17)
(The Prophet's composure amidst the manifestations): Just as He stated: (The heart lied not in what he saw) (verse 11), He stated here that during this vision, his sight neither swerved nor transgressed, but he observed everything with complete composure and focus. 'Zagha' means to swerve—meaning his sight did not deviate from the correct angle of observation, but he observed everything correctly. 'Tagha' means to transgress—meaning although the manifestations were abundant, his sight did not become dazed, but he observed everything firmly.

(He certainly saw of the greatest signs of his Lord) (18)
(Observing the greatest signs of Allah): This describes his observations, showing that he observed some of the greatest signs of his Lord. Their details are not mentioned because they are beyond description and comprehension; but 'kubra' (greatest) shows they were superior to the signs of nature and the human soul observed daily. Commentators have referred this to his Miraj (Ascension), supported by Surah Al-Isra. Note that he observed His signs, and there is no mention of seeing Allah Himself. Now, keep the summary in mind as you read further:
1. This Qur'an which warns you of the Day of Recompense is a solid reality, not the nonsense of soothsayers fabricated of his own accord, but is a divine revelation brought by His most مقرب (near) angel.
2. The angel chosen for this mission is highly مقرب, trustworthy, powerful, and wise, and cannot commit any error or be influenced by any other spirit.
3. The Prophet saw him in his actual form twice: first on the high horizon, and second at the Lote Tree.
4. He taught the Prophet like a compassionate teacher, in a manner ensuring the Prophet heard and understood clearly.

2. Subject Matter of the Subsequent Verses (19-30)
Next, the opponents are warned: on what basis do you feel secure from the Recompense? Do you rely on the intercession of your assumed deities—Allat, Al-Uzza, and Manat? If so, these are merely names you have named, without any reality. The Recompense is certain, and cannot be countered by your desires. Everything is in Allah's authority, and no angel can speak without His permission. Those who named the angels with female names to rely on their intercession have fabricated this to escape their responsibilities, but it will not benefit them. Allah knows best who is guided and who is deviated. Read the verses.
3. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(So have you considered Al-Lat and Al-'Uzza, and Manat, the third—the other?) (19-20)
(Rebuking the Quraysh): The question is to express amazement, contempt, and light of their deities. Above, the attributes of Gabriel were mentioned—showing he is mighty in power and wise. Elsewhere, he is described as obeyed (muta') and secure in position. These are high masculine qualities, and the Quraysh are rebuked: 'How can you compare these noble angels with your deities—Allat, Al-Uzza, and Manat—whom you have named with female names, assuming they are His daughters!'
(The three high deities of the polytheists): The subsequent verses show they were the idols of the angels, whom the polytheists believed were His daughters who would intercede for them to obtain sustenance in this world and salvation in the Hereafter. They regarded their intercession as certain, saying: 'These are high deities, and their intercession is anticipated.'
There is no need to discuss which tribe worshipped which idol; their greatness was recognized by all. To maintain their leadership over Arabia, the Quraysh had gathered these idols in the Kaaba, and since their worshippers were numerous, they respected them most.
(The ranks of these deities): The Qur'an shows there was a difference in their ranks. Allat and Al-Uzza held the highest ranks, while Manat was below them; hence, she was described as: 'the third—the other'—meaning she belonged to the group, but was of a lower rank.
( Lailu-l-arab): Al-Lat is a modified form of Al-Ilahah (the goddess), which became Al-Lat in common usage. Some have interpreted it from 'latt' (kneading), claiming it was a person who prepared food for pilgrims and was worshipped after death, which is grammatically incorrect and contrary to the Qur'an. The Qur'an clarifies that these were the idols of the angels, named with female names. Al-Uzza is the feminine of Al-A'azz, meaning the mighty, drawn from His attribute Al-Aziz. Manat is derived from 'maniyyah' (desire), meaning the deity from whom desires are sought.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 19 and 20 was combined in the original text.]

(Is for you the male and for Him the female? That, then, is an unjust division) (21-22)
(Rebuking their division): These phrases are sarcastic—showing: you prefer sons for yourselves and loathe daughters, yet you have attributed daughters to Him. If you did not reflect on His uniqueness, you should at least have avoided attributing to Him what you dislike for yourselves. This is an unjust division (deezan)—meaning: contrary to justice. 'Daza' means he did injustice. Stating daughters belong to Him is contrary to His status, as if His status is inferior to yours.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 21 and 22 was combined in the original text.]

(They are not but [mere] names you have named, you and your forefathers, for which Allah has sent down no authority. They follow not except assumption and what [their] souls desire, and there has already come to them from their Lord guidance) (23)
(Names without reality): This explains the reality of these deities: they are merely names you and your forefathers have named, without any reality. (for which Allah has sent down no authority) - He has sent down no proof in their support, and following your ancestors is no proof. Proof would exist if they were supported by intellect, nature, or His messengers. Lacking any proof, they are your own fabrications. (They follow not except assumption and what [their] souls desire, and there has already come to them from their Lord guidance) - This describes their bad state: while He revealed His guidance (the Qur'an) to show them the path, they chose assumption (conjecture) and desires. Seeking desires and conjecture is dangerous, but doing so while His guidance is present is leading yourself to ruin.
(Every innovation is produced by desires): (and what their souls desire) (ma tahwa-l-anfus) points to the shirk they fabricated. Every innovation (bid'ah) is produced by some desire of the soul: when a person cannot fulfill the requirements of the truth, he tries to fabricate a way to escape them. Their assumed deities had no proof, but to escape the fear of the Recompense, Satan tempted them: 'These are His daughters; if you worship them, they will intercede for you.' Thus, they expected to receive success in this world and the Hereafter easily, without making any sacrifice. What else other than desires can be the basis of this mythology!

(Or is there for man whatever he desires? But to Allah belongs the Hereafter and the first [life]) (24-25)
(Desires cannot change realities): This expresses amazement and comments on their state: you can fabricate whatever philosophy you wish to please yourselves, but your desires will not change realities. When the reality manifests, you will see that your assumed deities will not benefit you, but everyone will face only his deeds. Note that like the polytheists, the Jews, Christians, and Muslims have also fabricated false hopes contrary to His Book. We explained the false hopes (amani) of the Jews and Christians under Surah Al-Baqarah. The Muslims also fell into their ways, believing that belonging to a certain family guarantees Paradise. Through 'man' (human), the Qur'an warned: His law will not change for anyone's desires. (But to Allah belongs the Hereafter and the first [life]) - both worlds are in His authority alone. No one can intercede without His permission, or alter His decisions.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 24 and 25 was combined in the original text.]

(And how many an angel is there in the heavens whose intercession will not avail at all except after Allah has permitted [it] to whom He wills and approves) (26) - This explains: while they rely on their deities, even the angels in the heavens cannot intercede without His permission. No one can speak without His permission, and once permitted, they will intercede only for those whom He approves.

(Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter name the angels with female names) (27)
(The authors of the polytheistic mythology): This identifies those who fabricated this mythology: those who do not believe in the Recompense. It is stated: they are the ones who named the angels with female names, claiming they are His daughters, whose intercession will save them in this world and the Hereafter.

(And they have thereof no knowledge. They follow not except assumption, and indeed, assumption avails not against the truth at all) (28)
(Shirk is based entirely on assumption): It is stated: they fabricated this mythology without any knowledge, but followed assumption and conjecture to please themselves. They do not know that assumption cannot replace the truth. When the truth manifests, they will realize they were dreaming.
(The difference between assumption and knowledge): 'Dhann' (assumption/conjecture) was contrasted with 'Al-Huda' (guidance) in verse 23. Here, it is contrasted with 'knowledge' (ilm) and 'truth' (haqq). Knowledge is attained through nature, intellect, and His revelation (which is the truth). What lacks these is assumption, which is completely false. Describing their mythology as devoid of knowledge, He declared it a baseless fabrication.

(So turn away from him who turns away from Our remembrance and desires not except the worldly life) (29)
(Instruction to ignore those who turn away): The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is instructed: ignore those who choose desires over guidance, assumption over knowledge, and falsehood over truth; you have communicated the message, and they are responsible for their end. 'Remembrance' (dhikr) refers to the Qur'an, which warns of the Hereafter. (And desires not except the worldly life) points to the cause of their aversion: they have made this world their only objective, and cannot reflect on anything else.

(That is their sum of knowledge. Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who strays from His way, and He is most knowing of who is guided) (30)
(The narrow vision of the worldly): That is: their knowledge is limited only to the outward aspect of this world, and they cannot reflect on the reality hidden behind it, which is the true objective of life. Denying the Hereafter makes this world a plaything, whereas His power and wisdom visible in it show He could not have created it in vain. 'That is their sum of knowledge' also points to their narrow vision—showing they are infatuated with the temporary pleasures of this world.
(Threat to the worldly): (Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who strays from His way, and He is most knowing of who is guided) - This comforts the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and threatens the opponents: ignore them; your Lord knows well those who have strayed and those who are guided, and He will deal with each accordingly. His knowledge and power encompass all, and no rebel can escape Him, nor will any believer be deprived of His mercy.

4. Subject Matter of the Subsequent Verses (31-55)
Next, this theme is explained and reinforced. First, He explains that the sovereignty of the heavens and earth belongs to Allah alone, without any partner who can interfere with His justice; hence, He will punish the wicked and reward the righteous. Those who rely on their intercessors or lineage, assuming themselves entitled to Paradise without faith and righteous deeds, will be cast into Hell. He will save only those who avoid major sins and indecencies. If any minor error occurs, Allah will forgive them, for His forgiveness is vast. Those who boast of their purity should remember that He knows best that they were created from dust and a drop of semen; hence, a creature created from such elements has no right to boast of its purity. Then, He expresses amazement at those who withhold their wealth, yet assume they have high ranks in the Hereafter based on their lineage to Abraham and Moses—forgetting that the primary teaching in their scriptures is that no one will bear another's burden, and everyone will face only their own deeds.
Next, He explains that joy, grief, life, death, provision, wealth, and poverty are in His authority alone; hence, He alone should be sought. Those who worshipped Sirius (the star of spring), assuming it blessed them with prosperity, should know that Allah is the Lord of Sirius. Finally, He refers to past nations to warn the Quraysh: learn a lesson from their end, and do not invite His wrath. Read the verses.
5. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(And to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth—that He may recompense those who do evil with what they have done and reward those who do good with the best [reward]) (31)
(No one associates in His sovereignty): This states the result of refuting shirk: the sovereignty of the heavens and earth belongs to Allah alone, and no one can interfere with His decisions. (that He may recompense...) - 'Li' (that) is to express the result—showing that those who rely on their partners are in deep delusion. He will surely punish the wicked, and no one can save them; and He will surely reward the righteous with the best reward, without needing any intercession. This also shows that the wicked will receive only what they did, without any increase; but the righteous will receive His favor and bounty in addition to their reward.

(Those who avoid the major sins and immoralities, only [committing] minor offenses. Indeed, your Lord is vast in forgiveness. He was most knowing of you when He produced you from the earth and when you were fetuses in the wombs of your mothers. So do not claim yourselves to be pure; He is most knowing of who fears Him) (32)
(His primary demand of man) It is stated: the righteous are those who avoid major sins (ithm) and immoralities (fawahish), and if any minor error occurs, it is like a passing touch. They might commit a minor error occasionally, but they do not make it their habit, and hasten to purify themselves through repentance.
'Al-Lamam' literally means visiting a place briefly. Mujahid and Ibn Abbas interpreted it as committing a minor error but then separating from it. The meaning is: man is not expected to be an angel. Committing an error occasionally is not far from him, but He demands that his faith is so alert that he does not allow sin to capture his life, but repents immediately. Allah will forgive such individuals, for His forgiveness is vast. Surah An-Nisa states:
(Repentance is only [accepted] by Allah for those who do wrong in ignorance and then repent soon after. It is those to whom Allah will turn in forgiveness, and Allah is ever Knowing and Wise. And of no effect is the repentance of those who [continue to] do evil deeds until, when death overcomes one of them, he says, 'Indeed, I have repented now,' or of those who die while they are disbelievers. For them We have prepared a painful punishment) (An-Nisa, 4:17-18).
'Ithm' refers to those sins that involve encroaching on others' rights and tyranny, and 'fawahish' refers to immoralities. Fearing major sins, their faith becomes so alert that they do not commit minor sins either. One who preserves a great trust will not commit a minor betrayal.
(Warning to those who expect Paradise without faith and deeds): This warns those who were steeped in sins but expected Paradise based on intercessors, ancestral pride, and lineage. They are warned: what benefits with Allah is faith and deeds, not these false supports.
We noted that the Quraysh and the People of the Book suffered from similar delusions. The Quraysh boasted of being descendants of Abraham and Ishmael and the guardians of the Kaaba, but they were warned in Surah At-Tawbah:
(Have you made the providing of water to the pilgrim and the maintenance of Al-Masjid Al-Haram equal to [the deeds of] one who believes in Allah and the Last Day and strives in the cause of Allah?) (At-Tawbah, 9:19)—showing that these physical services cannot replace faith and deeds, nor cover up their crimes. Similarly, the Jews boasted of being descendants of Abraham and Isaac, assuming they would not enter the Fire except for a few days. Jesus rebuked them: 'Do not boast of being descendants of Abraham; God can raise descendants for Abraham from these stones.' The Muslims followed their ways, calling themselves the forgiven nation. This is incorrect, as salvation depends on deeds, not on lineage.
(He was most knowing of you when He produced you from the earth and when you were fetuses in the wombs of your mothers. So do not claim yourselves to be pure; He is most knowing of who fears Him) - This warns them: do not boast of your purity. Allah knows well your creation from dust and a drop of semen; hence, a creature created from such elements has no right to boast of its purity, ignoring the need for deeds. Its explanation appeared under Surah Al-Ma'arij, and will be further discussed under Surah Ash-Shams.
(Refuting pantheistic assumptions): Note that this right-rejecting description of human creation deals a blow to pantheistic assumptions (wahdat-ul-wujood) which claim man is a part of Allah. If he were a part of Allah, would He describe him in this right-rejecting manner?
Furthermore, claiming purity based on mere existence is described as boasting. Some claim: 'I am pure, free of any defect; how great is my status!' Can a believer make such an arrogant claim? Under the influence of others, some introduced these myths into Islam, repeating them without understanding.

(Have you seen the one who turned away and gave a little and [then] withheld? Does he have knowledge of the unseen, so he sees?) (33-35)
(A parable for those who expect Paradise without spending): This portrays the character of those who withhold their wealth, yet assume they have high ranks in the Hereafter. It is asked: do they possess the knowledge of the unseen to see their reserved ranks?
Commentators have generally referred 'the one' (alladhi) to Walid bin al-Mughirah, narrating that he intended to accept Islam but an associate told him: 'If you fear the Hereafter, pay me a certain amount and I will take the responsibility to save you.' He agreed, but gave only a little and then withheld.
Although widely reported, this report has no authority. Second, even if true, its connection with these verses is not clear. Did the Qur'an rebuke him for not fulfilling his promise to pay a bribe!
Since 'alladhi' is general, commentators try to find a specific individual for it, fabricating some stories that do not fit the context.
We repeatedly show that 'alladhi' and 'allati' are used in some places for parables—meaning to portray the general character of a group by representing it as a specific figure, as in Surah An-Nahl, verse 92: (And do not be like she who untwisted her spun thread after it was strong, [unraveling it] into fibers).
Similarly, here, a general character of the misers is portrayed, in whose mirror Walid bin al-Mughirah, Abu Lahab, and all other wealthy misers could see their reflection—mocking their assumption that they possess high ranks without spending. 
(The general way of the misers): 'And gave a little and then withheld' (wa a'ta qaleelan-wa akda) - 'akda' is derived from 'al-kadyu-l-'afir' (a rock appearing before the digger, preventing him from digging further). This describes the misers: if they spend, they spend only a little, and then their miserliness closes their hands. If urged, they grow angry: 'How much can I spend!' as described in Surah Al-Balad: (He says, 'I have destroyed wealth in abundance') (90:6).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 33, 34, and 35 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 33, 34, and 35 was combined in the original text.]

(Or has he not been informed of what was in the scriptures of Moses, and of Abraham, who fulfilled [his obligations]? That no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another) (36-38)
(A warning to both the Quraysh and the People of the Book): That is: they are expecting Paradise based on ancestral pride and intercession; have they not read in the scriptures of Moses and Abraham that no soul will bear another's burden?
Note that this addresses primarily the Quraysh, and secondarily the People of the Book. The People of the Book claimed to follow both prophets, and the Quraysh claimed Abraham as their ancestor and leader; hence, both are mentioned with care.
Abraham is described as: 'who fulfilled [his obligations]' (alladhi waffa)—meaning he executed every command of his Lord and passed every trial with loyalty, as stated: (And [remember] when Abraham was tried by his Lord with commands and he fulfilled them) (Al-Baqarah, 2:124). This is a warning: they received their high status in return for their loyalty, but you have merely opened a shop of boasting based on their name.
(Answering a question): A question arises: He referred to 'the scriptures of Abraham'; did Abraham have a scripture? The answer is: in the technical sense, he had no scripture. His teachings were oral, preserved as traditions in both branches of his family, and documented later in the Torah. Thus, referring to his scriptures is correct.
(The primary teaching in their scriptures):
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 36, 37, and 38 was combined in the original text.]

([Which is] that no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another) - This is the teaching in their scriptures: no soul will bear another's burden, but everyone must bear his own. This refutes the false intercession they believed in. This teaching is documented repeatedly in the Torah and the Gospels, and we have cited the references.

(And that there is not for man except that [good] for which he strives, and that his effort is going to be seen, then he will be recompensed for it with the fullest recompense) (39-41)
This further explains the preceding point: everyone will face only their own deeds; it will not happen that one performs a virtue and another enjoys its fruit, or one commits an evil and another faces its punishment. The meaning is: if your ancestors were righteous, they will receive their reward, and it will not happen that you enter Paradise based on their deeds, as stated: (That was a nation which has passed on. It will have what it earned, and you will have what you have earned) (Al-Baqarah, 2:134). If lineage could save, Noah's son would have been saved, and Abraham's father would have been saved. Lot's wife was married to a prophet, but this did not save her. Conversely, Pharaoh's wife was married to an enemy of Allah, yet He praised her in Surah At-Tahrim. These are the closest relationships, yet those who lacked deeds could not benefit from them; so what of others!
(How one can benefit from another's virtue):
One can benefit from another's virtue in two ways: first, if it is based on the bond of faith—such as when a believer prays for his brother; second, if he had some share in that virtue—such as when he taught it, set an example, or provided its resources. In these cases, it is included in his own effort.
(An incorrect deduction):
Some have used this verse to argue that the Qur'an supports the socialist principle of receiving only in accordance with labor, but this is an inappropriate use of the verse. We will discuss socialism in its relevant places. Briefly, this world is based on trial, and the Hereafter is based on justice and favor. Allah did not distribute wealth in this world in accordance with labor, but tested people's patience and gratitude by giving some more and some less, and they will receive reward or punishment in accordance with this test.
(and that his effort is going to be seen, then he will be recompensed for it with the fullest recompense) - That is: no one should assume this is an empty threat; everyone's deeds will be displayed, and they will receive a full recompense. This comforts the believers and warns the deniers.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 39, 40, and 41 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 39, 40, and 41 was combined in the original text.]

(And that to your Lord is the finality) (42)
(The ultimate return is only to Allah): That is: the return of everyone is only to your Lord. No one should assume he has any other protector who can save him from His grasp, or before whom His decisions can be challenged. Everyone must present themselves before Him, and His decisions will be final.

(And that it is He Who makes [one] laugh and weep, and that it is He Who causes death and gives life, and that He created the two mates—the male and female—from a sperm-drop when it is emitted, and that [incumbent] upon Him is the next creation) (43-47) - This is the proof showing why the ultimate return is only to Allah. It is stated: He alone is the One Who makes one laugh and weep—meaning: He creates the causes of joy, and He creates the causes of grief. Ease and difficulty, benefit and harm, are in His authority alone; so on what basis can anyone else be your resort? Similarly, He causes death and gives life; so since no one else has any authority over life and death, why choose anyone else as your resort?
He created the male and female. It is not that one was created by Him and the other by someone else; or that sons are granted by Him and daughters by someone else. Since there is no such division, why choose anyone else as your resort?
Their creation is from a drop of semen emitted into the womb, after which no one knows how it will develop—whether it will be male or female, complete or incomplete, or what features it will receive. He alone knows this, Who nurtures it within the veils of darkness and brings it forth as male or female. Since all of this is in His authority alone, why pray to others for children? (And that upon Him is the next creation) - that is: since He creates everyone from a drop of water, why should His power find it difficult to recreate them? Its possibility is clear, and His justice requires it; hence, His wisdom requires His bringing a Day to resurrect everyone, test their deeds, and recompense them. This was also stated in Abraham's declaration of disassociation in Surah Ash-Shu'ara:
(Who created me, and He [it is who] guides me. And it is He who feeds me and gives me drink. And when I am ill, it is He who cures me. And who will cause me to die and then bring me to life, and who I aspire that He will forgive me my sin on the Day of Recompense.) (Ash-Shu'ara, 26:78-82).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 43, 44, 45, 46, and 47 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 43, 44, 45, 46, and 47 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 43, 44, 45, 46, and 47 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 43, 44, 45, 46, and 47 was combined in the original text.]

(And that it is He Who enriches and suffices, and that He is the Lord of Sirius) (48-49)
(Warning to the wealthy misers): That is: He alone is the One Who enriches and blesses someone with such abundance that he accumulates wealth. 'Aqna' is derived from 'qunyah', which means accumulated wealth. 'Aghna' refers to those who are no longer poor, and 'aqna' refers to those who have accumulated wealth. In verses 33-34, the character of those wealthy misers was described who expect Paradise without spending. This warns them that the wealth they enjoy is His favor, and Sirius has no share in it, as Allah is the Lord of Sirius. (Sirius) - 'Shi'ra' is the name of a star that rises in spring, which the polytheists regarded as blessed, attributing the prosperity of spring to it. A classical poet wrote:
(He provides warmth in winter, and when Sirius rises [in spring], he provides shade and coolness) - showing that they regarded the stars as their guides. This was discussed under: (By the star when it descends)—showing that these stars prostrate to Allah. This is not necessarily part of the reference to the scriptures, but is an independent point. It is common to make such additions to clarify the subject.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 48 and 49 was combined in the original text.]

(And that He destroyed the first Aad, and Thamud, and He did not spare [them], and the people of Noah before. Indeed, they were more unjust and oppressive) (50-52)
(Warning to the Quraysh through history): This warns the Quraysh: just as you are warned, Aad, Thamud, and the nation of Noah were warned by their messengers, but they ignored the warning, and He destroyed them completely. The meaning is: you will face the same end if you persist in your way. Allah's law is uniform, and your history is its witness.
Aad is described as 'the first Aad' because Thamud succeeded them and was known as 'Aad the Second'. 'Indeed, they were more unjust and oppressive' relates to all these nations—showing that their destruction was because they chose transgression. The causes of their destruction emerged from within themselves.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 50, 51, and 52 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 50, 51, and 52 was combined in the original text.]

(And the overturned cities He overthrew, and covered them with what He covered) (53-54)
This refers to the nation of Lot. 'Al-Mu'tafikah' (overturned cities) refers to the winds that turn the earth upside down, as a plow turns the soil. When a flood covers the earth with sand, it is also described in this manner. Similarly, a violent wind that covers the earth with sand and pebbles is 'mu'tafikah'.
Allah Almighty unleashed a violent wind upon the nation of Lot, which pelted them with stones and overturned their homes: (So each We seized for his sin; and among them were those upon whom We sent a storm of stones) (Al-Ankabut, 29:40) and: (So We made the highest part [of the city] its lowest and rained upon them stones of hard clay) (Al-Hijr, 15:74).
(And covered them with what He covered) - This style is used to describe a situation that is beyond description, meaning they were covered by what cannot be described in words.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 53 and 54 was combined in the original text.]

(Then which of the favors of your Lord, [O man], will you doubt?) (55)
(Rebuking the Quraysh): The address is to the Quraysh. Although the pronoun is singular, it refers to the entire group, to focus on every individual within the group. They are rebuked: 'These proofs of the Recompense—drawn from intellect, scriptures, and history—are before you; so which of your Lord's signs will you deny?'
'Ala'' is the plural of 'ala' (favor/sign/power). In our commentators' view, it means blessings, which is an incomplete meaning. It means wonders, signs, power, and wisdom. Since blessings fall under this, they are included, but translating it as 'blessings' everywhere is incorrect. This word appeared in Surah Ar-Rahman, and we will explain its meaning there in the light of classical Arabic. Our teacher discussed this in detail in his book on مفردات (vocabulary).

6. Subject Matter of the Subsequent Verses (56-62)
In the conclusion, as is common, the theme of the introduction is recalled—namely: do not regard this Qur'an as the nonsense of soothsayers, but as a divine revelation. Just as there were warners in past times, this is a warner. What it warns you of is near, and He alone can remove it. Do not mock it; it is a subject for weeping, not laughing. Take heed; prostrate to Allah and worship Him. Read the verses.
7. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(This is a warner of the former warners) (56) - 'This' (hadha) refers to the Qur'an. Since the Surah began with the Qur'an, He referred to it at the end to clarify the coherence. If anyone refers it to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the meaning remains close. In some places, the words: (a reminder, a messenger) appeared—using 'reminder' for the Qur'an, and 'messenger' for the Prophet. But the style is such that both appear as one. 'Nudhur' is the plural of 'nadheer' (warner), referring to past prophets and scriptures, and warning them: do not regard this as a joke; if you mock it, you will face the end past nations faced.

(The Approaching [Day] has approached. No one besides Allah can remove it) (57-58)
(Stating an absolute reality): 'Azifah' means that which is near, referring here to the punishment of which the Qur'an warns. The meaning is: do not regard the punishment as distant; it is hovering over your heads. We explain that when a messenger comes, the nation is granted respite only as long as necessary to establish the proof, after which they are destroyed if they persist in denial. This is an absolute reality. (No one besides Allah can remove it) - do not assume your deities will save you; He alone can remove it.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 57 and 58 was combined in the original text.]

(Then at this statement do you wonder? And you laugh and do not weep) (59-60)
This expresses amazement at their condition: the Book is informing you of a near punishment, yet you are wondering how it can come! Know that this is a subject for weeping, not laughing, but you are laughing!
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 59 and 60 was combined in the original text.]
(While you are heedless?) (61) - 'Samidoon' (or samood) means to be dazed and heedless—meaning: while the warning is trying to awaken you, you are sleeping in heedlessness.

(So prostrate to Allah and worship [Him]) (62) - That is: if you desire safety, awaken, abandon your deities, prostrate to your Lord, and worship Him. No one besides Him can save you. The Surah began with the setting and prostration of stars, and concludes with the instruction to prostrate to Allah and worship Him. All praise belongs to Allah for completing the commentary of this Surah. Rahmanabad, July 23, 1977 / Sha'ban 6, 1397 AH.
